mappings.rationale: Directly addresses the philosophy of religion, covering the existence and attributes of the divine, problems of evil and free will, and arguments for and against God's existence — all core topics of section 811.A.

Covers theology as an attempt to understand and state the rationale of religious belief, including doctrines concerning God, revelation, and the relationship between faith and reason — central to 811.D.

Directly addresses questions about the nature and existence of the divine across religious traditions, covering monotheism, polytheism, pantheism, and other doctrines concerning God outlined in 811.D.3.
Provides a broad overview of the study and classification of religions, addressing the nature and characteristics of religion itself — directly relevant to 811.A and 811.E.
Addresses atheism and its philosophical foundations, directly relevant to 811.D.3.d on atheism and agnosticism as doctrinal positions regarding the divine.
Examines agnosticism as a philosophical position on knowledge of the divine, directly relevant to 811.D.3.d and 811.A.1.c on the limits of human knowledge of the sacred.
Covers myth as a system of belief and its relationship to religion, directly relevant to 811.F.1 on myth and mythology as other systems of belief.
Addresses religious experience and mystical theology, covering the nature and varieties of spiritual experience outlined in 811.B and 811.D.2.
Directly addresses the philosophical problem of free will, one of the special problems listed in 811.A.1.d in the context of philosophy of religion.
Addresses the problem of evil, one of the special problems of philosophy of religion identified in 811.A.1.d concerning evil and suffering.
Directly addresses miracles as a topic in philosophy of religion and theology, relevant to 811.D.11 on miracles.
Directly covers the relationship between religion and science, a key topic in 811.G.2.
Engages with fundamental questions about existence, purpose, and the sacred that connect to the basic questions and problems of philosophy of religion in 811.A.1.
Addresses the relationship between morality and religion, relevant to 811.G.4 on religion and morality.
Examines the nature of religious faith, articles of faith, and the epistemology of belief, directly relevant to 811.D.1.c on religious creeds and 811.B on religious experience.
Explores the relationship between religion and society, including debates about religion's public role, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Examines fundamentalism as a religious phenomenon across traditions, relevant to 811.E on the study and classification of religions and 811.D on doctrinal positions.
Explores humanist alternatives to religious belief, relevant to 811.D.3.d on atheism and agnosticism and 811.G on religion's relation to other aspects of human experience.
Covers magic as a system of belief and practice, directly relevant to 811.F.2 on magic as an alternative belief system.
Addresses witchcraft as a system of belief and practice, directly relevant to 811.F.3 on witchcraft.
Examines superstitious beliefs and their relationship to religious thought, relevant to 811.F on other systems of belief including magic and nature worship.
Covers pagan belief systems including nature worship, polytheism, and ancestor worship, relevant to 811.F.6-8 and 811.D.3.a on polytheism.
Provides a comparative survey of world mythologies, directly relevant to 811.F.1 on myth and mythology as systems of belief.
Examines Greek and Roman mythological traditions as belief systems, relevant to 811.F.1 on myth and mythology.
Addresses philosophical scepticism including scepticism about religious knowledge, relevant to 811.A.1.c on the extent to which mankind can know the divine.
Examines concepts of the sacred and its violation across religions, relevant to 811.A on the nature of the divine and 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Provides comparative study of Judaism, Christianity, and Islam, directly relevant to 811.E on the study and classification of religions.
Addresses fundamental questions about existence, causation, and the nature of reality that underpin philosophy of religion topics in 811.A, including the existence of the divine.
Directly addresses angelology across religious traditions, the specific topic of 811.D.5.
Covers doctrines of religious dualism and the problem of evil through the figure of the Devil, relevant to 811.D.3.b on religious dualism and 811.A.1.d on evil.
Covers the theory of interpretation including biblical and theological hermeneutics, relevant to 811.D.1.a on the role of Sacred Scriptures.
Explores the relationship between conscience, morality, and religion, relevant to 811.G.4 on religion and morality.
Covers anthropological approaches to religion, ritual, and belief systems, relevant to 811.C on religious phenomenology and 811.E on the classification of religions.
Examines religious toleration and its philosophical foundations, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
Explores the relationship between religion and warfare, relevant to 811.G on religion in relation to other aspects of human experience.
Illustrates the relationship between religion and art, relevant to 811.G.1 on religion and art.
Explores existentialist philosophy's engagement with questions of God, meaning, and faith (Kierkegaard, Sartre), relevant to 811.A on philosophy of religion.
Kierkegaard is a foundational figure in philosophy of religion, addressing faith, religious experience, and the leap of faith, relevant to 811.A and 811.B.
Augustine is central to theology and philosophy of religion, addressing doctrines of grace, free will, evil, and creation outlined in 811.D.
Aquinas is a foundational figure in philosophical theology, addressing proofs for God's existence, divine attributes, and natural theology — core topics of 811.A and 811.D.
Anselm's ontological argument for God's existence is a central topic in philosophy of religion, directly relevant to 811.A.1.a.
Hume's philosophy includes influential critiques of arguments for God's existence, miracles, and natural religion, relevant to 811.A.1 and 811.D.11.
Spinoza's pantheistic philosophy directly relates to 811.D.3.c.iii on pantheism and panentheism.
Kant's philosophy addresses the limits of religious knowledge and moral arguments for God's existence, relevant to 811.A.1.c and 811.G.4.
Examines polygamy's relationship to religious beliefs and practices across traditions, relevant to 811.G.3 on religion and society.
